source.messages:
Connor O'Brien: Retro thread for the first Runtime LTS patch release (1.18.1-lts). Drop what went well/poorly + action items. Keep it concrete please.

Talia Benson: +1. I’ll consolidate into the retro doc afterwards. If you can, include: impact, root cause, and what we’ll change before 1.18.2-lts.

ReleaseBot: Release complete: runtime 1.18.1-lts tagged and artifacts published. Checksums + provenance uploaded. (ref: release-manager checklist)

Sahana Iyer: Went well: release branch CI was mostly deterministic. The new “release/*” workflow caught the toolchain mismatch before publish.
Sahana Iyer: Not great: secrets scope for signing keys is still too broad. We had to temporarily elevate perms for the runner, which felt sketchy.
Sahana Iyer: Action: I can split signing into a separate job that runs only on a locked runner pool + requires manual approval (GitHub env protection). ETA early next week.

Devon Price: +1 on deterministic-ish CI. The cherry-pick helper saved time overall.
Devon Price: Pain: conflicts still took a while because the script doesn’t preflight “will this apply cleanly” before opening the backport PR. We had 3 backport PRs opened that were doomed from the start.
Devon Price: Action: add a `--dry-run` mode that attempts the cherry-pick locally in a temp branch and posts conflict summary to the PR template. I’ll take it.

Marcus Reed: Perf gates: the new perf-canary “release branch” suite worked, and it prevented us from shipping the batching heuristic change (would’ve been noisy).
Marcus Reed: But we burned ~6 hours chasing the p95 regression that turned out to be runner contention + cold GPU caches. The signal wasn’t actionable without reruns.
Marcus Reed: Action: require 2 consecutive runs for p95 regressions within 1–2% of threshold, and annotate runs w/ runner load + GPU warmup status. Kira V / I can implement.

Kira Volkov: Agree. We should bake the “rerun policy” into the gate summary output so release managers aren’t making it up in Slack.
Kira Volkov: Action: update perf gate summary artifact to include: baseline hash, runner ID, contention metric, and “needs rerun?” suggestion based on variance band. I’ll pair w/ Marcus.

Sean Gallagher: SRE perspective: rollout monitoring was clean once we had the dashboards open. No incident, no page. :thumbsup:
Sean Gallagher: Gaps: we didn’t have a crisp “mandatory vs optional” patch call. Support asked, and we kind of hand-waved “recommended.” For enterprise, we need consistent language.
Sean Gallagher: Action: propose criteria + template for “mandatory” (security / Sev1 crash / data loss) vs “optional” (perf improvements, minor correctness). I’ll draft and share for approval.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also, escalation path during patch week was fuzzy. We had 3 different threads (eng-runtime, eng-releases, eng-sre) and people weren’t sure where to ask.
Rafael Mendes: Action: add a single “LTS patch war room” channel or at least a pinned checklist: where to post perf gate failures, where to post signing issues, who is current RM. I can own w/ Talia.

Elliot Price: Release notes: “LTS mode” generator was a big win. The taxonomy (Fixes / Perf / Known issues / Compatibility) landed well with CS.
Elliot Price: What didn’t: we still had manual edits for compatibility matrix + upgrade notes, and we almost shipped without mentioning the experimental batching heuristic being disabled (important for expectation setting).
Elliot Price: Action: add required metadata fields in PR labels (e.g., `compat-impact: none|low|med|high`, `upgrade-note`) and have the generator fail if missing for LTS-approved PRs.

Dev Patel: Support view: comms were late. We got the patch link after customers were already asking.
Dev Patel: Also, the “how to verify checksums” snippet was buried. We need a short Support-ready response template with links.
Dev Patel: Action: I’ll write a canned response for “patch available” + “how to request backport” and get Connor/Elliot to review.

Rachel Kim: From pilot accounts: release notes format was good, but they asked for a single “What changed vs previous LTS patch” section at the top.
Rachel Kim: They also asked “what models / GPUs were perf-tested” (even just a short list).
Rachel Kim: Action: can we add a standard “Test coverage summary” block to the notes? I can provide the customer wording; need eng to supply the content reliably.

Allison Grant: Observability: we had good dashboards but too much manual clicking.
Allison Grant: Action: add a “release link bundle” in the checklist: dashboards, logs query, perf gate artifact, known issues page, artifact provenance. Make it a single pasteable thing.

Bruno Silva: Private packaging: pinning worked for the installer, but the version string mapping was confusing (1.18.1-lts vs runtime image tag vs helm values). We had to answer the same question twice.
Bruno Silva: Action: add a “Version naming cheat sheet” section to the release notes + Private upgrade guide. I can draft with Elliot.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side: freezing autotune defaults did what we wanted (less variance). One hiccup: we didn’t document the override path clearly; someone asked how to revert per workload.
Hiro Tanaka: Action: add a short “Autotune defaults in LTS” note in known issues / config docs, include example env var / config flag.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler regressions: the new smoke suite caught one edge case in RC. That saved us.
Sofia Ivanova: But it ran late in the pipeline, so we found it after we thought we were “green.”
Sofia Ivanova: Action: move scheduler smoke tests earlier (after unit tests, before full artifact build). Sahana/Devon I can help wire.

Connor O'Brien: This is great. Let me summarize key wins:
- CI mostly deterministic; release branch workflows are real now
- Perf gate exists and blocked risky changes
- Release notes generator in LTS mode is usable
- Pilot rollout had no pages

Connor O'Brien: Key pain points I’m hearing:
- Signing key permissions + job isolation
- Perf gate flake handling / rerun policy
- Cherry-pick conflicts still too manual
- Comms timing (Support/CS) + clearer “mandatory” language
- Version naming confusion for Private/Helm
- Release notes still require manual edits (compat + “what changed”)

Talia Benson: Action item list (draft) — reply with corrections/owners:
1) Isolate signing job + tighten secrets scope (Sahana) — target: before 1.18.2-lts RC
2) Cherry-pick helper dry-run + conflict summary (Devon) — target: before next patch week
3) Perf gate rerun policy + richer metadata in summary artifact (Marcus + Kira V) — target: 2 weeks
4) Define “mandatory vs optional” patch criteria + template (Sean) — target: next week
5) Create/pin “war room” process + single place for patch coordination (Rafael + Talia) — target: before next patch
6) Release notes generator: enforce required metadata + fail-fast for missing upgrade/compat tags (Elliot) — target: 2 weeks
7) Support canned responses + verification snippet surfaced (Dev Patel w/ Elliot review) — target: next week
8) Add “What changed vs previous patch” + “Test coverage summary” blocks in release notes template (Rachel + Elliot + Marcus) — target: next patch
9) Version naming cheat sheet for Private/Helm (Bruno + Elliot) — target: next patch
10) Scheduler smoke tests earlier in CI (Sofia + Sahana) — target: next patch RC
11) Document LTS autotune defaults + override path (Hiro) — target: next patch
12) Release link bundle in RM checklist (Allison) — target: next week

Connor O'Brien: Looks right. One more: we should codify the “hold release” decision tree. We hesitated when the kv-cache crash fix landed late.
Connor O'Brien: Action: add decision tree to RM checklist: what blocks release, what can be waived, who signs off. I’ll own.

Elliot Price: For (6)/(8) — +1. I’ll update the notes template so those blocks exist even if empty, and the generator can prompt for them.

Sahana Iyer: For (1)/(10): OK. I’ll create issues and tag Sofia.

Marcus Reed: For (3)/(8): yep, I’ll add a short “bench coverage” output: workloads + GPU SKUs + commit hash.

Rafael Mendes: For (5): I prefer not creating a new channel yet. Maybe a pinned “Patch Week Thread” in eng-releases with links + explicit @here for on-point.
Rafael Mendes: Let’s do that first, and if it’s still messy, we make a dedicated channel.

Talia Benson: Works. I’ll draft the pinned “Patch Week Thread” template.

Dev Patel: For (7): I’ll post the canned response in #support-internal for feedback and then link it here.

Connor O'Brien: Perfect. I’ll convert these into Linear tasks and link back. Thanks everyone. :thumbsup:

source.content:
Connor O'Brien: ok let’s publish 1st LTS patch. target: runtime `1.18.1-lts`. need: artifacts + checksums + release notes links (Confluence + docs) so Support/CS can forward.

Sahana Iyer: CI is green on `runtime-1.18-lts` and rc tag. build pipeline produced signed artifacts + sha256. waiting on final “publish” step from release job.

Devon Price: I can kick the publish job once we confirm paths + naming. do we want canonical URLs under `artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/`?

Connor O'Brien: yep. canonical:
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/
- keep `latest` symlink OFF for now (don’t want people auto-upgrading).

Elliot Price: release notes are generated and ready to post. Confluence draft is here: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRV/Runtime+LTS+1.18.1-lts+Release+Notes (still in “Draft”).

Marissa Cole: for customer-facing, we need docs site link too (Console will link docs, not Confluence). can we publish to docs in parallel?

Elliot Price: yep. docs PR already merged (redwood-docs). should be live at:
https://docs.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts
(assuming the docs deploy finished ~5 min ago)

ReleaseBot: Docs deploy completed: redwood-docs@deploy-1442 (prod). URL: https://docs.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts

Connor O'Brien: perfect. Sahana/Devon can we paste the artifact list + sha256 file URL here once publish runs?

Devon Price: starting publish job now. will post the manifest output.

Allison Grant: quick check: perf-gate summary attached to release notes? at least link it for internal folks.

Connor O'Brien: internal only is fine. add under “Verification” section in Confluence; docs can just say “perf regression gates passed” w/out raw numbers.

Sahana Iyer: publish job is running… also SBOM generation is included. outputs:
- `runtime-1.18.1-lts-provenance.json`
- `runtime-1.18.1-lts-sbom.spdx.json`
- `sha256sums.txt`
- `sha256sums.txt.sig`

Dev Patel: for Support: we need a single message we can copy into tickets w/ (1) version tag (2) release notes link (3) checksums (4) private/helm pinning instructions. can we include those all in docs page?

Elliot Price: docs page includes “Install / Pinning” blocks for Helm + Private. also a “Verify downloads” section referencing sha256sums + signature.

Bruno Silva: confirming Private installer references:
- image tag: `registry.redwood.ai/runtime:1.18.1-lts`
- installer pin: `runtime.imageTag: 1.18.1-lts`
- checksum verification step pulls `sha256sums.txt` from artifacts url.

Sean Gallagher: SRE ask: please post rollout note in #eng-sre once it’s live, include dashboards link for lts traffic so we can watch errors/latency while pilot customers pick it up.

Devon Price: publish job done. artifact index:
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/runtime-1.18.1-lts-linux-amd64.tar.gz
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/runtime-1.18.1-lts-linux-amd64.tar.gz.sig
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/sha256sums.txt
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/sha256sums.txt.sig
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/runtime-1.18.1-lts-provenance.json
- https://artifacts.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts/runtime-1.18.1-lts-sbom.spdx.json

Devon Price: container images pushed:
- `registry.redwood.ai/runtime:1.18.1-lts`
- `registry.redwood.ai/runtime:1.18.1-lts-cuda12`
- `registry.redwood.ai/runtime:1.18.1-lts-rocm6` (pilot only)

Sahana Iyer: checksums sanity check:
`sha256sums.txt` includes entries for tarball + provenance + sbom.
also signature verifies against release key `redwood-release@redwood.ai`.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: everyone please double-check the sha line for the tarball matches local download before we tell Support.

Elliot Price: Confluence page published (no longer Draft). same link:

Connor O'Brien: perf gate flaked again on the 1.18.1-lts RC. Gate says p95 latency regression > threshold on A100. Anyone else looking at the perf-canary artifact?

perf-canary-bot: ❌ LTS Perf Gate: FAILED
Branch: runtime-1.18-lts
Candidate: rc-2025-03-05-1
Workload: chat-mix-v2 (seq_len mixed)
GPU: A100-80GB
Metric: p95 end-to-end latency
Result: +3.4% regression (threshold: +2.0%)
Artifact: https://perf-canary.redwood.dev/runs/1741192871

Connor O'Brien: It passed on H100 and L40S, and throughput deltas are basically noise. This one is just p95 A100. Before we hit the panic button: is this runner contention again?

Sahana Iyer: CI side: runner pool for perf is super hot right now. Queue depth jumped after we merged the release-branch workflow changes. I see 4 other perf jobs sharing the same bare metal host group. Not supposed to, but the scheduler is packing.

Marcus Reed: Looking at the run details now. The p50 is actually slightly better (-0.6%). p95 is where it blows up. That pattern (p50 ok, p95 worse) is usually tail latency from noisy neighbor / CPU steal / IO hiccup vs a real kernel change.

Claire Dubois: +1. Also check if we had a different prompt distribution on this run. The artifact says “chat-mix-v2”, but sometimes the seed changes and the long-tail prompts amplify noise.

Marcus Reed: Seed looks consistent with the last passing run. But the host telemetry panel shows occasional spikes in “host cpu wait” and “disk read latency”. Not huge, but aligned with the p95 spikes.

Jonas Weber: Reminder: we tightened p95 thresholds for LTS in perf-canary PR-427. We’re now at +2.0% for p95 on that workload. That’s fine, but it also means we need an explicit anti-flake policy (rerun count / median) or we’re going to keep burning release days.

Connor O'Brien: For *this* RC: do we rerun and proceed if it clears? Or do we hold and investigate deeper? We’re trying to publish today.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: I’m ok with a rerun *if* we can attribute the failure to infrastructure noise. But I don’t want “rerun until green” as the implicit policy. We need one controlled rerun on a known-clean runner pool.

Allison Grant: Checking the perf runner dashboards: around the failure window there’s a spike in GPU host interrupts and CPU iowait on the A100 pool. Also another long-running job started ~3 min before the p95 went sideways. So it’s plausible contention.

Rafael Mendes: If we ship with a real p95 regression we’ll hear about it fast (Dedicated customers are super sensitive). I’d support rerun once on a drained host group, and if it fails again, we block.

Devon Price: Mechanically we can force a rerun on the “perf-a100-dedicated” label (the smaller pool, less shared). It’ll take ~45 min to start though.

Kira Volkov: Another data point: the failure run has higher variance than usual. Standard deviation on p95 looks ~2x compared to baseline. That’s usually infra noise. I can paste numbers if helpful.

Connor O'Brien: Please do. I want something we can link in the release thread so it’s not vibes.

Kira Volkov: From artifact:
- Baseline p95: 412ms (run-to-run stddev ~3.1ms historically)
- This run p95: 426ms
- Within-run stddev: 9.4ms (normal is ~4-5ms)
So the point estimate is +3.4%, but the run itself is noisy.

Claire Dubois: That within-run stddev is a red flag for measurement stability.

Jonas Weber: Proposal for today:
1) Trigger rerun on dedicated A100 runner pool.
2) If rerun <= +2.0% => accept and proceed.
3) If rerun still > +2.0% => block release and open a perf investigation issue.
Longer term: change gate to “median of 3” or “2 consecutive failures required”.

Connor O'Brien: +1. Sahana/Devon can we do #1 without messing with anything else? No code changes, just rerun.

Sahana Iyer: Yep. I can kick a rerun with the dedicated label and also pause new submissions to that pool while it runs. Give me ~5.

Devon Price: I’ll start a note for an action item: perf-canary should auto-detect noisy hosts and mark as “invalid run” rather than hard fail. But that’s a bigger change.

Sean Gallagher: Also we should capture this in the runbook: “Allowed rerun count: 1 (on isolated pool). Waiver requires SRE + Release Eng approval.”

Connor O'Brien: Agree. No waivers for perf unless we can explain. :thumbsup:

perf-canary-bot: 🔁 Rerun requested by Sahana Iyer
Queue: perf-a100-dedicated
New run: https://perf-canary.redwood.dev/runs/1741194210

Marcus Reed: While that runs: any chance the new scheduler regression tests are stealing CPU on these hosts? I saw some overlap in time windows.

Sahana Iyer: The scheduler tests run on separate CI runners, not on the perf hosts. But the perf hosts share a control plane service with other jobs (artifact upload, etc). Still shouldn’t cause this, but possible.

Allison Grant: Another thought: the failure window aligns with the backup job on the perf NFS volume (weekly snapshot). That would explain IO latency spikes. I’ll ping infra after this thread.

Connor O'Brien: That would be… very on brand.

Jonas Weber: If that’s the culprit, we should schedule snapshots away from release windows OR move perf inputs local.

Rafael Mendes: For the record: if we need to block, we message Support/CS that the patch is delayed due to perf gate investigation. But hopefully rerun clears.

perf-canary-bot: ✅ LTS Perf Gate: PASSED (rerun)
Branch: runtime-1.18-lts
Workload: chat-mix-v2
GPU: A100-80GB
Metric: p95 end-to-end latency
Result: +1.1% regression (threshold: +2.0%)
Artifact: https://perf-canary.redwood.dev/runs/1741194210

Marcus Reed: Nice. p50/p95 both look normal and variance is back to expected range.

Connor O'Brien: Ok, we proceed with publish. I’m going to note in the release thread that the initial fail was infra noise; rerun on dedicated pool passed.

Sean Gallagher: +1 proceed. But we need follow-ups:
- identify NFS snapshot / contention root cause
- define rerun policy in perf gates doc + runbook

Allison Grant: I’ll open an infra ticket re: snapshot timing + noisy neighbor alerts for perf pools.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll open an internal issue to prevent perf runners from being packed + add an admission control during patch week.

Jonas Weber: I’ll draft a small change to the gate logic: require 2 consecutive failures before hard-fail OR automatically run 2 and take best/median (we can bikeshed, but current is too sensitive).

Connor O'Brien: Action items recap:
- Allison: infra ticket re snapshot / IO contention
- Sahana: runner packing / scheduling guardrails
- Jonas: gate anti-flake proposal
I’ll add a note to the retro doc. Thanks all.

Connor O'Brien: RC -> GA done. Starting Hosted canary (5%) now; will post greenlight/hold status in ~60 min.

Connor O'Brien: Need to sanity-check our out-of-band security patch procedure for runtime LTS before we’re in the fire. Can folks confirm the exact steps + approvals + comms + artifact verification? Thinking for 1.18-lts.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. Also want clarity on what we can do “fast path” vs what still can’t be skipped (signing/provenance).

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security POV: we can accelerate reviews + testing scope, but we can’t skip provenance, checksums, and “who approved what” audit trail. Also no patch without CVE/issue tracker reference (even if embargoed, use the internal ID).

Connor O'Brien: Proposed emergency flow:
1) Security fix lands on main (private security PR if embargo)
2) Backport PR to `runtime-1.18-lts`
3) Cut `1.18.x-lts` hotfix patch
4) Publish artifacts + release notes + notify Support/CS/Sales

Where I’m fuzzy: who must approve the backport PR + who’s allowed to trigger signing keys in CI.

Sahana Iyer: CI/signing: only Release Eng on-call + build on-call have access to the release branch secrets. For emergency, I can grant time-boxed access to Connor/Devon if needed, but better is to have me run the “publish” workflow.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE: we need a go/no-go checkpoint before publish. Even in emergency, we need at least:
- smoke + scheduler regression suite green
- minimal perf canary (we can accept reduced matrix w/ explicit waiver)
- rollback plan + monitoring dashboards ready

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also specify who declares it “mandatory” vs “optional” for Dedicated/Hosted rollouts. That affects incident response / comms.

Connor O'Brien: Good call. Let’s encode:
- Release Manager (me or Release Eng on-call) drives
- Security sponsor (Aisha or delegate) confirms severity + disclosure constraints
- SRE on-call manager (Rafael/Sean) signs off on readiness

Hanae Suzuki: Approvals: I want explicit in-thread:
- Security: Hanae (coordination) + Aisha (policy sponsor) or named delegate
- Runtime owners: Mateo (exec) not in every patch, but for emergency: at least one of Noah/Hiro/Sofia depending on area
- Release Eng: Connor + Sahana (build)

Connor O'Brien: Ok. For the backport PR itself, does “two-ship” apply? (Runtime + Security)

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Yes. Emergency doesn’t mean single-approver. Minimum:
- 1 Security approval (Aisha/Hanae)
- 1 Runtime approval (Noah/Hiro/Sofia)
- 1 Release Eng approval (Connor or Release Eng on-call)
SRE approval is a release readiness gate, not required to merge the PR.

SecurityBot: Reminder: For embargoed vulns, do not reference customer names, exact exploit details, or public CVE links in Slack. Use internal ticket IDs and mark notes as “SECURITY SENSITIVE”.

Dev Patel: Support angle: we need a quick “what customers will see” + “what to ask for” update. Even if details are minimal, we need:
- affected versions
- symptoms/impact
- mitigation if customer can’t patch same day
- when patched artifacts are available (ETA)

Elliot Price: Release notes format: for LTS hotfix, we should still generate the slower-change notes but the security section goes at the top. We can do:
- Security Fixes (CVE-XXXX or “Redwood Security Advisory RSA-####”)
- Known Issues
- Compatibility/Upgrade Notes
And explicitly: “No other changes intended.”

Connor O'Brien: Let’s get concrete: artifact verification steps. What’s the exact “verify” checklist we want everyone to run before saying “published”?

Sahana Iyer: Current best practice (what I’d put in runbook):
A) Verify tag points at expected commit
B) Verify image digest matches release manifest
C) Verify cosign signature + provenance attestation
D) Verify checksums for tarballs
E) Verify SBOM artifact exists (even if minimal)

Sahana Iyer: Commands we use:
- Tag/commit:
  `git show runtime-1.18.2-lts --no-patch --pretty=fuller`
- Checksums:
  `sha256sum -c runtime-1.18.2-lts-checksums.txt`
- Cosign (keyless):
  `cosign verify --certificate-identity "https://github.com/redwood/.../.github/workflows/release.yml@refs/tags/runtime-1.18.2-lts" --certificate-oidc-issuer "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" <IMAGE_REF>@<DIGEST>`
- Provenance:
  `cosign verify-attestation --type slsaprovenance <IMAGE_REF>@<DIGEST>`

Sean Gallagher: + add “pull + run healthcheck” on a canary node, even if tiny:
- start runtime container
- run a fixed prompt smoke test
- confirm no crash loops

Rafael Mendes: And we should capture the verification output (paste the command output / attach artifact) in the release thread for audit.

Connor O'Brien: For perf gates: do we have an explicit reduced matrix for emergency security patches?

Sean Gallagher: Suggest:
- 1 GPU SKU (most common for LTS customers) + 1 model class
- latency p95 within threshold OR explicitly waived with rationale
- throughput sanity (no catastrophic drop)
If perf is unknown, we can still ship but must mark “mandatory only for security; may impact perf; rollback guidance included” and notify CS.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security policy: it is acceptable to ship with reduced perf validation if exploitation risk is high and mitigations are limited. But we must document that as a waiver and follow up with full perf run within 48h.

Connor O'Brien: Ok. Comms list:
- #support (Dev)
- #customer-success (Rachel)
- #sales (Avery/Hannah)
- Product (Marissa/Paula)
- For customers: dedicated email template via CS + ticket for Private accounts

Avery Johnson: Please give Sales a single paragraph we can reuse. Something like: “Out-of-band LTS security patch available; no feature changes; recommended upgrade; artifact verification instructions.” Also whether it changes support window/cadence (it shouldn’t).

Rachel Kim: CS needs:
- which accounts are on LTS pilot
- whether patch is mandatory for them
- whether they can stay pinned until next scheduled patch if low risk

Hanae Suzuki: Mandatory vs optional criteria: if it’s remotely exploitable / data exposure -> mandatory. If it’s local-only w/ mitigation -> recommended.

Connor O'Brien: Who makes the mandatory call?

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security sponsor makes recommendation; SRE + Release Eng confirm feasibility of rollout; Product/CS informed. Final call = Security sponsor.

Dev Patel: Support request: if we say mandatory, we need clear steps for:
- Hosted/Dedicated: “we will roll forward” timeline + maintenance window expectations
- Private: “download/pin to X” + checksums + rollback steps

Sahana Iyer: Private artifact verification should include the exact checksum file link + image digest. If Private installer is used, it should verify checksums automatically, but customers will still ask for manual steps.

Elliot Price: Release notes should include:
- “How to verify artifacts” short section with the cosign command
- “Compatibility”: “no config changes expected” (unless there are)
- “Upgrade/Rollback”: link to Private guide

Connor O'Brien: Drafting the emergency checklist now. Anything else “must not forget”?

Sean Gallagher: Monitoring: before rollout, ensure the LTS dashboards are on the incident rotation + alerts are sane. If we flip Hosted/Dedicated first, we watch 30–60 min before widening.

Rafael Mendes: Also add: create an internal incident-style channel if patch is in response to active exploitation. Otherwise, keep it in #eng-releases + security channel.

Hanae Suzuki: One more: disclosure coordination. If there’s a CVE, ensure we don’t publish details before upstream embargo lifts. Release notes may have to say “Security fix included; contact support for details” until disclosure date.

Connor O'Brien: Got it. Proposed emergency patch SOP (v0):
- Trigger: Sev0/Sev1 vuln (per Security)
- Create security backport PR to `runtime-1.18-lts` (labels: `lts-approved`, `security`, `backport`)
- Approvals: Security + Runtime + Release Eng
- CI: release-branch workflow + scheduler regression suite + minimal perf canary (waiver allowed)
- Build artifacts: deterministic build, generate checksums, SBOM, provenance
- Verify: tag->commit, sha256, cosign signature, provenance attestation, pull+smoke
- Publish: artifact bucket + docs link + release notes w/ security section
- Comms: Support/CS/Sales + customer advisory (template)
- Rollout: Hosted/Dedicated staged; Private notify + pin instructions

Any objections?

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Looks good. Add “recordkeeping”: link to internal security ticket + approvers list in the release PR body.

Sahana Iyer: + ensure signing key access is via workflow, not manual. If the workflow fails due to secrets, ping me; don’t try to workaround locally.

Dev Patel: Please include a one-liner for Support: “If customer asks if they’re affected, request runtime version + deployment type + whether they use Private installer.”

Avery Johnson: And note: “LTS cadence remains monthly; this is an exception for security.”

Sean Gallagher: Approved from SRE side as long as perf waiver requires explicit Rafael/Sean ack.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Put “waiver must be pasted in thread + reason + follow-up action owner/time.”

Connor O'Brien: Done. I’ll convert this into the runbook section and paste a final checklist in the repo checklist doc. Thanks all.

Hanae Suzuki: Please also add a “do not post” reminder (no exploit strings/logs) in the checklist.

Connor O'Brien: Will do.

SecurityBot: Note: If you need to share sensitive reproduction details, use the security ticket and mark attachments “confidential”. Avoid Slack uploads.

source.messages:
Connor O'Brien: Hey folks — looking for Product/CS eyes on the proposed Runtime LTS release notes taxonomy before we bake it into the generator. What would you *expect* to see as an enterprise reader vs what’s noise?

Connor O'Brien: Current draft sections: Summary, Security fixes (CVE), Critical fixes, Other fixes, Perf notes, Upgrade notes, Compatibility matrix, Known issues/workarounds, Artifact verification.

Elliot Price: Link to draft template in Confluence (WIP): /eng-serving-runtime/release-notes/runtime-lts-release-notes-format-and-taxonomy :point_up:

Marissa Cole: Skimmed — structure is solid. Main feedback: we need a VERY explicit “What changed for me?” at the top.
Marissa Cole: For enterprise folks reading at 2am: 1) should I upgrade 2) what will break 3) how do I roll back.
Marissa Cole: Suggest a top banner like:
- Impact: Optional / Recommended / Mandatory
- Affected customers: Hosted/Dedicated/Private
- Time to apply + any required restarts

Paula Mendes: +1. Also add “Customer-visible behavior changes” as a first-class subsection (even if empty most patches).
Paula Mendes: Right now the “Other fixes” bucket could hide behavior changes (e.g., batching heuristic tweaks). We should force it to be called out.

Rachel Kim: From CS side: Compatibility matrix is a must, but it needs to be *actionable*.
Rachel Kim: Like: Runtime 1.18.1-lts compatible with:
- Private installer >= X
- Helm chart >= Y
- NVIDIA driver >= Z (if relevant)
- Supported GPU SKUs list
Rachel Kim: Otherwise we get a bunch of “does this work with our stack?” tickets.

Jasmine Liu: Also please include “Known issues introduced in this patch” vs “Known issues still present”. Customers read “Known issues” and assume it’s all old.
Jasmine Liu: Even a line like “No new known issues in this patch” is reassuring.

Dev Patel: Support asks:
1) Put release date + timezone at the top (we copy/paste into Zendesk macros)
2) Add “How to get help” / escalation pointer (support channel + what data to include)
3) In known issues, include symptom + quick mitigation + whether backport planned + target patch if known
Dev Patel: This reduces ping-pong.

Soojin Lee: Marketing-ish but still important: define LTS terms inline once.
Soojin Lee: Enterprise buyers are picky about what “LTS” means. Recommend short box: support window, patch cadence, backport policy headline (“security + Sev1/Sev2 only”), no feature promises.
Soojin Lee: Helps Sales/CS forward the notes without extra context.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if we say “Mandatory” we need criteria. Otherwise it becomes political.
Sean Gallagher: Could be “Mandatory if: security critical, Sev1 crash/data corruption, SLO impact observed in prod, or active exploitation.”
Sean Gallagher: And include “Operational risk” callout: e.g., “This patch changes scheduler queueing behavior under overload.”

Allison Grant: On perf notes: please include the exact benchmark set + deltas + noise caveats.
Allison Grant: Something like:
- Workloads: chat-8k, summarization-2k, mixed-lens
- GPUs: A100-80G, H100
- Metrics: p50/p95 latency, tok/s
- Result: within +/- X% vs baseline
Allison Grant: Even if summarized, a link to the perf-canary artifact is gold.

Marissa Cole: Another thing: enterprise customers ask “what am I pinned to?”
Marissa Cole: Can the release notes include the *Git commit* / tag + artifact digests? (Even if the full verification steps are elsewhere.)

Elliot Price: Yep, template has “Release identifiers” but we can make it more prominent:
- git tag
- commit SHA
- container image tags
- sha256 checksums
- SBOM link (when available)

Quentin Harris: Re: compatibility matrix — we should keep it short but consistent. Proposed fields:
- Runtime version
- API compatibility (same as mainline 1.18)
- Model compat notes (if any)
- Private installer min
- Helm chart min
- CUDA/driver range (only when it changes)
Quentin Harris: I can own keeping that matrix accurate per patch.

Connor O'Brien: This is super helpful. A couple follow-ups:
1) Do we want separate “Dedicated ops notes” vs “Private ops notes”?
2) How detailed should “Other fixes” be? We’re trying to stay slower-change.

Rachel Kim: Separate ops notes: yes, but keep it minimal.
Rachel Kim: Private readers need steps (upgrade sequencing + rollback). Dedicated readers mostly need “no customer action” vs “requires rolling restart” and “expected impact window.”

Dev Patel: For “Other fixes” — as long as you label customer impact, it’s fine.
Dev Patel: We don’t need every internal refactor. But if it changes logs, metrics names, default flags, or failure modes, we need it.

Paula Mendes: Maybe enforce a rule: every line item has a “Customer impact: none / low / medium / high” tag.
Paula Mendes: If we can’t say impact, we shouldn’t ship it into LTS.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: to impact tags. Also add “Monitoring changes” if any dashboards/alerts need updates.

Allison Grant: +1. If a metric name changes, it’s not “Other.” That’s an ops change.

Soojin Lee: Wording suggestion: avoid “bugfixes” as a bucket. Use “Fixes” + “Known limitations.” Lawyers get nervous about “bug free” vibes.

Marissa Cole: Also: people want to know what *didn’t* change.
Marissa Cole: For LTS, a short “Stability statement” helps: “No kernel autotune default changes” / “No scheduler policy changes” unless otherwise noted.

Connor O'Brien: Love that. We can add a “Stability / invariants” block:
- Kernel autotune defaults: unchanged
- Scheduling policies: unchanged
- Default batching heuristics: unchanged

- Add top banner: Impact + audience + restart requirements
- Split known issues into new vs existing
- Add explicit customer-visible behavior changes section

title: #eng-releases: 1741083310
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Hey folks — looking for Product/CS eyes on the proposed Runtime LTS release notes taxonomy before we bake it into the generator. What would you *expect* to see as an enterprise reader vs what’s noise?

Connor O'Brien: Current draft sections: Summary, Security fixes (CVE), Critical fixes, Other fixes, Perf notes, Upgrade notes, Compatibility matrix, Known issues/workarounds, Artifact verification.

Elliot Price: Link to draft template in Confluence (WIP): /eng-serving-runtime/release-notes/runtime-lts-release-notes-format-and-taxonomy :point_up:

Marissa Cole: Skimmed — structure is solid. Main feedback: we need a VERY explicit “What changed for me?” at the top.
Marissa Cole: For enterprise folks reading at 2am: 1) should I upgrade 2) what will break 3) how do I roll back.
Marissa Cole: Suggest a top banner like:
- Impact: Optional / Recommended / Mandatory
- Affected customers: Hosted/Dedicated/Private
- Time to apply + any required restarts

Paula Mendes: +1. Also add “Customer-visible behavior changes” as a first-class subsection (even if empty most patches).
Paula Mendes: Right now the “Other fixes” bucket could hide behavior changes (e.g., batching heuristic tweaks). We should force it to be called out.

Rachel Kim: From CS side: Compatibility matrix is a must, but it needs to be *actionable*.
Rachel Kim: Like: Runtime 1.18.1-lts compatible with:
- Private installer >= X
- Helm chart >= Y
- NVIDIA driver >= Z (if relevant)
- Supported GPU SKUs list
Rachel Kim: Otherwise we get a bunch of “does this work with our stack?” tickets.

Jasmine Liu: Also please include “Known issues introduced in this patch” vs “Known issues still present”. Customers read “Known issues” and assume it’s all old.
Jasmine Liu: Even a line like “No new known issues in this patch” is reassuring.

Dev Patel: Support asks:
1) Put release date + timezone at the top (we copy/paste into Zendesk macros)
2) Add “How to get help” / escalation pointer (support channel + what data to include)
3) In known issues, include symptom + quick mitigation + whether backport planned + target patch if known
Dev Patel: This reduces ping-pong.

Soojin Lee: Marketing-ish but still important: define LTS terms inline once.
Soojin Lee: Enterprise buyers are picky about what “LTS” means. Recommend short box: support window, patch cadence, backport policy headline (“security + Sev1/Sev2 only”), no feature promises.
Soojin Lee: Helps Sales/CS forward the notes without extra context.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if we say “Mandatory” we need criteria. Otherwise it becomes political.
Sean Gallagher: Could be “Mandatory if: security critical, Sev1 crash/data corruption, SLO impact observed in prod, or active exploitation.”
Sean Gallagher: And include “Operational risk” callout: e.g., “This patch changes scheduler queueing behavior under overload.”

Allison Grant: On perf notes: please include the exact benchmark set + deltas + noise caveats.
Allison Grant: Something like:
- Workloads: chat-8k, summarization-2k, mixed-lens
- GPUs: A100-80G, H100
- Metrics: p50/p95 latency, tok/s
- Result: within +/- X% vs baseline
Allison Grant: Even if summarized, a link to the perf-canary artifact is gold.

Marissa Cole: Another thing: enterprise customers ask “what am I pinned to?”
Marissa Cole: Can the release notes include the *Git commit* / tag + artifact digests? (Even if the full verification steps are elsewhere.)

Elliot Price: Yep, template has “Release identifiers” but we can make it more prominent:
- git tag
- commit SHA
- container image tags
- sha256 checksums
- SBOM link (when available)

Quentin Harris: Re: compatibility matrix — we should keep it short but consistent. Proposed fields:
- Runtime version
- API compatibility (same as mainline 1.18)
- Model compat notes (if any)
- Private installer min
- Helm chart min
- CUDA/driver range (only when it changes)
Quentin Harris: I can own keeping that matrix accurate per patch.

Connor O'Brien: This is super helpful. A couple follow-ups:
1) Do we want separate “Dedicated ops notes” vs “Private ops notes”?
2) How detailed should “Other fixes” be? We’re trying to stay slower-change.

Rachel Kim: Separate ops notes: yes, but keep it minimal.
Rachel Kim: Private readers need steps (upgrade sequencing + rollback). Dedicated readers mostly need “no customer action” vs “requires rolling restart” and “expected impact window.”

Dev Patel: For “Other fixes” — as long as you label customer impact, it’s fine.
Dev Patel: We don’t need every internal refactor. But if it changes logs, metrics names, default flags, or failure modes, we need it.

Paula Mendes: Maybe enforce a rule: every line item has a “Customer impact: none / low / medium / high” tag.
Paula Mendes: If we can’t say impact, we shouldn’t ship it into LTS.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: to impact tags. Also add “Monitoring changes” if any dashboards/alerts need updates.

Allison Grant: +1. If a metric name changes, it’s not “Other.” That’s an ops change.

Soojin Lee: Wording suggestion: avoid “bugfixes” as a bucket. Use “Fixes” + “Known limitations.” Lawyers get nervous about “bug free” vibes.

Marissa Cole: Also: people want to know what *didn’t* change.
Marissa Cole: For LTS, a short “Stability statement” helps: “No kernel autotune default changes” / “No scheduler policy changes” unless otherwise noted.

Connor O'Brien: Love that. We can add a “Stability / invariants” block:
- Kernel autotune defaults: unchanged
- Scheduling policies: unchanged
- Default batching heuristics: unchanged
(And if we deviate, it’s called out.)

Elliot Price: Action items I’m capturing:
- Add top banner: Impact + audience + restart requirements
- Split known issues into new vs existing
- Add explicit customer-visible behavior changes section
- Make identifiers (tag/SHA/digests) prominent
- Perf notes: structured summary + link to perf-canary artifact
- Add escalation/support pointer + required diagnostics
- Add stability/invariants block

Connor O'Brien: Great — I’ll update the template + feed these requirements into ENG-4826 (release notes generator LTS mode). Thanks all. :pray:

Rachel Kim: When you update, tag me — I’ll sanity check from “what will my customer ask next?” perspective.

Dev Patel: Same, happy to review final version + draft a Support macro based on the top banner.

Marissa Cole: :+1: Once it’s stable we’ll align on how Console links/render it (PM-914).

source.text:
Connor O'Brien: seeing a weird one: release branch CI failing but main is green. Branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` (cut yesterday). Failing job: `release-ci / build-runtime-images`.

Connor O'Brien: link: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/actions/runs/9142218832

CI Bot: Workflow run failed: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Sahana Iyer: pulled logs. it’s dying during toolchain bootstrap + then again when trying to upload artifacts.

Sahana Iyer: excerpt:
```
==> Step: Setup toolchain
+ ./ci/install_toolchain.sh
Detected branch: runtime-1.18-lts
Using toolchain manifest: ci/manifests/toolchain-release.yaml
Installing clang-17...
E: Unable to locate package clang-17

==> Step: Build
ninja: error: loading 'build.ninja': No such file or directory

==> Step: Publish checksums
+ ./ci/publish_checksums.sh
remote: Permission to redwood/runtime-artifacts denied to github-actions[bot].
fatal: unable to access 'https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts/': The requested URL returned error: 403
```

Devon Price: two separate issues then: runner image doesn’t have apt repo for clang-17, AND token perms for publish step.

Sean Gallagher: why is it only on release branch? main uses the same workflow right?

Sahana Iyer: not exactly. main uses `toolchain-main.yaml` + self-hosted runner group `perf-ci-large`. release branches use `ubuntu-22.04` hosted runners + `toolchain-release.yaml` to keep it “stable”.

Connor O'Brien: ok that explains clang-17 missing. release manifest probably points at 17 but runner’s apt sources don’t have llvm repo configured.

Kira Volkov: also the 403: on release branches we tightened default permissions last week (contents: read). publish script pushes to `runtime-artifacts` repo, so it needs a PAT or workflow permission with `contents: write` + access to that repo.

Rafael Mendes: (reaction: :eyes:) do we have secrets available on release branches? sometimes GH blocks org secrets on non-default branches depending on env rules.

Sahana Iyer: yep, looks like `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` is empty in that run. log line:
```
WARNING: RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN not set; falling back to GITHUB_TOKEN
```
then 403.

Devon Price: I think the secret is scoped to `main` environment only. release workflow uses `environment: release` but the env doesn’t include that secret.

Connor O'Brien: so fix should be:
1) pin toolchain install to our prebuilt toolchain container (no apt)
2) move artifact publish to use `release` environment with the PAT + allow it on release branches

Allison Grant: quick check: this is blocking perf gate artifacts too? if publish fails we won’t get perf-canary summary attached.

Sahana Iyer: yep, `publish_checksums.sh` is in the same job right before it uploads the build manifest + checksums.

Sean Gallagher: for immediate unblock, can we rerun with manual override / skip publish?

Connor O'Brien: would rather not skip; enterprises will expect checksums/provenance even for RCs. but we can get a green build first to validate branch.

Devon Price: I can push a workflow hotfix: add llvm apt repo step for release branches as a short-term patch, then follow up w/ container pin.

Sahana Iyer: +1 short term. add:
```
- name: Add LLVM apt repo
  run: |
    wget -O - https://apt.llvm.org/llvm-snapshot.gpg.key | sudo apt-key add -
    sudo add-apt-repository "deb http://apt.llvm.org/jammy/ llvm-toolchain-jammy-17 main"
    sudo apt-get update
```
then `apt-get install clang-17` should work.

Kira Volkov: re token: we should not rely on `GITHUB_TOKEN` cross-repo. safest is to use `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` PAT with least privilege on that repo.

Rafael Mendes: can we confirm why secret not available? GH settings: env `release` requires approval? could be pending.

Sahana Iyer: checked run summary: no “Waiting for approval” banner. It’s just not present. likely never added to `release` env.

Connor O'Brien: @Sahana can you add the token to `release` environment + restrict to `runtime-*-lts` branches?

Sahana Iyer: I don’t have admin perms for env secrets. we need someone from Infra or repo admin.

Devon Price: I can ping Infra (INT-3302 was about release branch secrets, might still be open). @Rafael can you help?

Rafael Mendes: yep, I can update env secrets. what’s the secret name exactly?

Connor O'Brien: `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` (PAT for https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts). Also `COSIGN_KEY_RELEASE` later but not in this job yet.

Rafael Mendes: ok.

CI Bot: Re-run requested by Connor O'Brien (attempt #2)

Devon Price: I pushed a tiny workflow tweak in my branch but can’t land it to release branch until CI is green… catch-22.

Sahana Iyer: we can cherry-pick the workflow change directly onto the release branch (workflows are allowed). it’ll trigger a new run.

Connor O'Brien: do it. minimal diff only.

Devon Price: ok cherry-picking now.

Devon Price: pushed commit to `runtime-1.18-lts`: `ci: add llvm apt repo for clang-17 on hosted runners`.

CI Bot: Workflow run started: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Rafael Mendes: updated `release` environment with `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` and allowed branch pattern `runtime-*-lts`. should be available on next run.

Sahana Iyer: (reaction: :pray:) watching logs.

Sahana Iyer: toolchain step now passes. clang installs.

Sahana Iyer: publish step is now using the PAT (no warning) and push succeeded:
```
Pushing checksums to runtime-artifacts...
Enumerating objects: 14, done.
To https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts
   8a21c1d..c3f11a0  lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1 -> lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1
```

Allison Grant: nice. does it also upload the provenance json?

Sahana Iyer: yep, artifact list includes:
- `runtime-1.18.1-rc1+g<sha>.tar.zst`
- `checksums.txt`
- `provenance.json`
- `build-manifest.txt`

Sean Gallagher: can we add a note to runbook: release branches require `release` env secret setup + llvm repo, until container pin lands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, I’ll update the runbook + we should file a follow-up to move release branches to toolchain container so we don’t depend on apt repos.

Devon Price: I’ll open a ticket and link here. also, we should flip the workflow so it fails fast if `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` missing (no silent fallback).

Connor O'Brien: agreed.

CI Bot: Workflow run succeeded: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Connor O'Brien: thanks all. action items:
- [Devon] follow-up PR: use toolchain container for release branches; remove apt install.
- [Rafael] confirm env secret restrictions are correct and documented.
- [Sahana] add guardrail: fail if token missing; update checklist/runbook.

Connor O'Brien: also reran the original failed run and marked as obsolete in the thread. :thumbsup:

Connor O'Brien: also reran the original failed run and marked as obsolete in the thread. :thumbsup:
text:
Connor O'Brien: seeing a weird one: release branch CI failing but main is green. Branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` (cut yesterday). Failing job: `release-ci / build-runtime-images`.

Connor O'Brien: link: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/actions/runs/9142218832

CI Bot: Workflow run failed: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Sahana Iyer: pulled logs. it’s dying during toolchain bootstrap + then again when trying to upload artifacts.

Sahana Iyer: excerpt:
```
==> Step: Setup toolchain
+ ./ci/install_toolchain.sh
Detected branch: runtime-1.18-lts
Using toolchain manifest: ci/manifests/toolchain-release.yaml
Installing clang-17...
E: Unable to locate package clang-17

==> Step: Build
ninja: error: loading 'build.ninja': No such file or directory

==> Step: Publish checksums
+ ./ci/publish_checksums.sh
remote: Permission to redwood/runtime-artifacts denied to github-actions[bot].
fatal: unable to access 'https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts/': The requested URL returned error: 403
```

Devon Price: two separate issues then: runner image doesn’t have apt repo for clang-17, AND token perms for publish step.

Sean Gallagher: why is it only on release branch? main uses the same workflow right?

Sahana Iyer: not exactly. main uses `toolchain-main.yaml` + self-hosted runner group `perf-ci-large`. release branches use `ubuntu-22.04` hosted runners + `toolchain-release.yaml` to keep it “stable”.

Connor O'Brien: ok that explains clang-17 missing. release manifest probably points at 17 but runner’s apt sources don’t have llvm repo configured.

Kira Volkov: also the 403: on release branches we tightened default permissions last week (contents: read). publish script pushes to `runtime-artifacts` repo, so it needs a PAT or workflow permission with `contents: write` + access to that repo.

Rafael Mendes: (reaction: :eyes:) do we have secrets available on release branches? sometimes GH blocks org secrets on non-default branches depending on env rules.

Sahana Iyer: yep, looks like `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` is empty in that run. log line:
```
WARNING: RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN not set; falling back to GITHUB_TOKEN
```
then 403.

Devon Price: I think the secret is scoped to `main` environment only. release workflow uses `environment: release` but the env doesn’t include that secret.

Connor O'Brien: so fix should be:
1) pin toolchain install to our prebuilt toolchain container (no apt)
2) move artifact publish to use `release` environment with the PAT + allow it on release branches

Allison Grant: quick check: this is blocking perf gate artifacts too? if publish fails we won’t get perf-canary summary attached.

Sahana Iyer: yep, `publish_checksums.sh` is in the same job right before it uploads the build manifest + checksums.

Sean Gallagher: for immediate unblock, can we rerun with manual override / skip publish?

Connor O'Brien: would rather not skip; enterprises will expect checksums/provenance even for RCs. but we can get a green build first to validate branch.

Devon Price: I can push a workflow hotfix: add llvm apt repo step for release branches as a short-term patch, then follow up w/ container pin.

Sahana Iyer: +1 short term. add:
```
- name: Add LLVM apt repo
  run: |
    wget -O - https://apt.llvm.org/llvm-snapshot.gpg.key | sudo apt-key add -
    sudo add-apt-repository "deb http://apt.llvm.org/jammy/ llvm-toolchain-jammy-17 main"
    sudo apt-get update
```
then `apt-get install clang-17` should work.

Kira Volkov: re token: we should not rely on `GITHUB_TOKEN` cross-repo. safest is to use `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` PAT with least privilege on that repo.

Rafael Mendes: can we confirm why secret not available? GH settings: env `release` requires approval? could be pending.

Sahana Iyer: checked run summary: no “Waiting for approval” banner. It’s just not present. likely never added to `release` env.

Connor O'Brien: @Sahana can you add the token to `release` environment + restrict to `runtime-*-lts` branches?

Sahana Iyer: I don’t have admin perms for env secrets. we need someone from Infra or repo admin.

Devon Price: I can ping Infra (INT-3302 was about release branch secrets, might still be open). @Rafael can you help?

Rafael Mendes: yep, I can update env secrets. what’s the secret name exactly?

Connor O'Brien: `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` (PAT for https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts). Also `COSIGN_KEY_RELEASE` later but not in this job yet.

Rafael Mendes: ok.

CI Bot: Re-run requested by Connor O'Brien (attempt #2)

Devon Price: I pushed a tiny workflow tweak in my branch but can’t land it to release branch until CI is green… catch-22.

Sahana Iyer: we can cherry-pick the workflow change directly onto the release branch (workflows are allowed). it’ll trigger a new run.

Connor O'Brien: do it. minimal diff only.

Devon Price: ok cherry-picking now.

Devon Price: pushed commit to `runtime-1.18-lts`: `ci: add llvm apt repo for clang-17 on hosted runners`.

CI Bot: Workflow run started: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Rafael Mendes: updated `release` environment with `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` and allowed branch pattern `runtime-*-lts`. should be available on next run.

Sahana Iyer: (reaction: :pray:) watching logs.

Sahana Iyer: toolchain step now passes. clang installs.

Sahana Iyer: publish step is now using the PAT (no warning) and push succeeded:
```
Pushing checksums to runtime-artifacts...
Enumerating objects: 14, done.
To https://github.com/redwood/runtime-artifacts
   8a21c1d..c3f11a0  lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1 -> lts/runtime-1.18.1-rc1
```

Allison Grant: nice. does it also upload the provenance json?

Sahana Iyer: yep, artifact list includes:
- `runtime-1.18.1-rc1+g<sha>.tar.zst`
- `checksums.txt`
- `provenance.json`
- `build-manifest.txt`

Sean Gallagher: can we add a note to runbook: release branches require `release` env secret setup + llvm repo, until container pin lands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, I’ll update the runbook + we should file a follow-up to move release branches to toolchain container so we don’t depend on apt repos.

Devon Price: I’ll open a ticket and link here. also, we should flip the workflow so it fails fast if `RUNTIME_ARTIFACTS_TOKEN` missing (no silent fallback).

Connor O'Brien: agreed.

CI Bot: Workflow run succeeded: release-ci / build-runtime-images (runtime-1.18-lts)

Connor O'Brien: thanks all. action items:
- [Devon] follow-up PR: use toolchain container for release branches; remove apt install.
- [Rafael] confirm env secret restrictions are correct and documented.
- [Sahana] add guardrail: fail if token missing; update checklist/runbook.

Connor O'Brien: also reran the original failed run and marked as obsolete in the thread. :thumbsup:

source.content:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off initial LTS branch cut today. Goal: create `runtime-1.18-lts` and get an RC artifact built + staged (not public) by EOD.

Connor O'Brien: Checklist draft (reply w/ owner + status):
1) Confirm cut point commit on main (and freeze window)
2) Create branch + protections
3) Update versioning (1.18.0-lts?) + tagging scheme
4) CI for release branch (secrets, perms, deterministic build)
5) Kernel autotune defaults freeze (LTS)
6) Scheduler smoke/regression suite enabled for release branches
7) Perf-canary gate run + review (p50/p95 thresholds)
8) Artifact: build + checksums/provenance stub
9) Private installer pinning sanity + Helm values sanity
10) Release notes generator LTS mode dry run

Talia Benson: I’ll track action items + post status summary in-thread every ~2 hrs. If something blocks, please call it out explicitly w/ who you need.

Sahana Iyer: On (4) CI + secrets/perms. I’m seeing release/* branch workflow can’t access signing key vault in staging yet. I’ll open internal ticket but might need @Devon Price to adjust the pipeline.

Devon Price: Yep I can pair. We can gate signing to “staging-only unsigned” for RC if keys aren’t ready. But we should still produce checksums.

Mateo Alvarez: For (1) cut point: please use commit after the scheduler starvation fix merge? I don’t want LTS starting before that.

Sofia Ivanova: starvation fix (main) merged late last night. commit is `9b3a1c2` (approx). I’ll confirm we don’t pull in the follow-on refactor.

Connor O'Brien: +1. Preference is minimal diff surface. If the refactor is in same area, we can leave it out and backport only the fix.

Hiro Tanaka: On (5) kernel autotune freeze: PR is ready to cherry-pick once branch exists. It pins autotune params to reduce variance. I can be owner.

Marcus Reed: On (7) perf-canary: I can run the first set once build artifacts exist. Note runner capacity is tight; if we need reruns, we should reserve GPUs now.

Sean Gallagher: SRE note: for the first RC, we at least need latency + error baseline on the canonical workloads. If we ship RC without perf gates we’ll get destroyed later.

Connor O'Brien: Agreed. No publish until (7) has a clean pass OR explicit waiver w/ SRE signoff.

Kira Volkov: On (10) release notes generator LTS mode: I can do a dry run after branch exists. Need label taxonomy final: `lts/backport`, `lts/security`, `release-note:perf`, etc.

Elliot Price: I’ll sanity-check the output format (compat matrix, known issues block, upgrade notes). For first patch we want it “slower-change” style.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll be backup for release manager approvals. Also: call out who’s “on point” if CI goes red on release branch (it tends to be different than main).

Connor O'Brien: On point for CI issues: @Sahana Iyer (primary) @Devon Price (automation). I’ll stay in loop.

Talia Benson: Status board (live):
(1) Cut point commit: Sofia
(2) Branch + protections: Connor
(3) Version/tag scheme: Connor + Elliot
(4) CI secrets/perms: Sahana + Devon
(5) Kernel autotune freeze: Hiro
(6) Scheduler regression suite: Sofia
(7) Perf canary: Marcus + Sean
(8) Artifact checksums/provenance: Sahana
(9) Private installer/Helm sanity: Devon (loop in Bruno if needed)
(10) Release notes generator: Kira + Elliot

Sofia Ivanova: (1) confirmed cut point: main @ `9b3a1c2` includes starvation fix only (no follow-on refactor). Good to cut here.

Connor O'Brien: (2) Creating branch `runtime-1.18-lts` now.

buildkite-bot: Pipeline started: redwood/runtime :: create-branch-protections

Connor O'Brien: Branch is created. Protections: require PR review + required checks (unit, integration, scheduler-smoke, perf-canary placeholder). Admin override disabled.

Sahana Iyer: (4) Release branch workflow failing at `publish-artifacts` step: "permission denied: write to s3://redwood-artifacts-staging/runtime/". Looks like missing IAM role for release branches.

Devon Price: I can patch the pipeline to assume role based on branch prefix `runtime-*-lts`. Might be quickest. Longer term we should standardize `release/*`.

Connor O'Brien: Do it. For today, let’s unblock by allowing `runtime-*-lts` to publish to staging only.

Sean Gallagher: + please ensure we don’t accidentally publish to prod bucket. Staging-only is fine.

Devon Price: ack. I’ll add explicit guardrail: if bucket != staging, fail hard.

Hiro Tanaka: (5) Cherry-pick ready: "freeze kernel autotune defaults". Opening backport PR to `runtime-1.18-lts`.

buildkite-bot: PR opened: runtime-1.18-lts <- cherry-pick/pr-23241 (kernel autotune defaults)

Sofia Ivanova: (6) Scheduler regression suite: I need to flip the CI config flag for release branches. There’s a test job that’s disabled by branch filter. I’ll patch in the branch pattern.

Sahana Iyer: (8) checksums/provenance: I can add `sha256sum` generation in pipeline even if signing key isn’t available yet. Provenance metadata can include commit SHA + build ID.

Elliot Price: For LTS release notes, we’ll want artifact checksums referenced in the notes. Even if not public, keep the template consistent.

Connor O'Brien: (3) versioning proposal: branch baseline `1.18.0-lts` and first patch will be `1.18.1-lts`. RC tag `1.18.1-lts-rc1`.

Mateo Alvarez: ok. Just ensure customer-facing semantics: “LTS patch == stable, rc == internal/pilot only.”

Kira Volkov: (10) Labels: can we standardize now?
- `lts/backport-candidate`
- `lts/approved`
- `lts/security`
- `release-note:perf`
- `release-note:bugfix`
- `release-note:known-issue`

Connor O'Brien: Works. We can start with those and refine later.

buildkite-bot: Pipeline updated by Devon Price: release-branch-publish-staging-only guard enabled

Sahana Iyer: Re-running build.

buildkite-bot: Build #11872 started on branch runtime-1.18-lts

Marcus Reed: While that runs: perf plan for first gate:
- Workloads: chat mixed-length (p50/p95), long-context, embeddings
- GPUs: A100 + H100
- Thresholds: no >2% p50 regression, no >3% p95 regression vs baseline; throughput no worse than -2%.
If we’re noisy, we rerun once. Beyond that needs waiver w/ explanation.

Sean Gallagher: Add also error rate + OOM incidence. We’ve been bitten by “perf ok but crashy.”

Marcus Reed: yep, will include.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler job enabled on branch filter. Opening PR to release branch config.

buildkite-bot: PR opened: enable scheduler-smoke on runtime-1.18-lts

Connor O'Brien: Quick check: any “must exclude” features from mainline before we cut RC? (experimental batching heuristic, etc)

Hiro Tanaka: Experimental batching heuristic is currently on main but guarded behind a config. I’d prefer we disable by default on LTS. That’s separate PR though.

Mateo Alvarez: Agree with disable on LTS. Predictability > a couple % throughput.

Connor O'Brien: Not required for branch cut, but let’s track it as next PR before RC.

Talia Benson: Adding (11) Disable experimental batching default on LTS (owner: Hiro + Sofia for scheduler interactions). Target: before RC build.

buildkite-bot: Build #11872 finished: SUCCESS. Artifacts published to staging. Checksums generated.

Sahana Iyer: Great. Staging artifacts are up. No signing yet; checksums file is in the artifact bundle.

Marcus Reed: I can kick perf-canary now. Need the image tag / artifact URI.

Sahana Iyer: Tag is `runtime:1.18.1-lts-rc1` in staging registry. I’ll DM exact URI.

Sean Gallagher: Please also link the buildkite run so I can cross-check env + kernel versions.

Sahana Iyer: link: https://buildkite.example/redwood/runtime/builds/11872 (internal)

Kira Volkov: Running release notes generator in LTS mode against current branch. I need a list of included PRs (at least kernel freeze + scheduler smoke enable).

Connor O'Brien: For RC1, minimal: baseline branch cut + kernel freeze + scheduler smoke CI enable. We’ll add batching-disable before RC2.

Elliot Price: Also add known-issues placeholder section even if empty. Enterprise folks expect it.

Kira Volkov: generator output looks ok, but it wants `compatibility_matrix.md` path. We don’t have that in-repo yet. Can I stub with “see Console/docs link TBD” for pilot?

Connor O'Brien: Stub is fine for RC. Add TODO to create proper matrix doc before GA.

Rafael Mendes: Reminder: approval workflow for changes on LTS branch—need at least Runtime owner + Release Eng. For RC we should still practice it.

Connor O'Brien: Yep. For each PR to `runtime-1.18-lts`: 1 Runtime approver (Mateo delegate ok), 1 Release Eng (me/Sahana), CI green, scheduler-smoke green.

Sofia Ivanova: Kernel freeze PR is green; I approved as Runtime (scheduling side). Waiting for Connor.

Connor O'Brien: Approved.

buildkite-bot: PR merged into runtime-1.18-lts: freeze kernel autotune defaults

Hiro Tanaka: I’ll prep the batching-disable PR next.

Talia Benson: Status summary:
- Branch created + protections: done
- CI publish to staging: unblocked + guardrails in place
- Checksums: generated (signing pending)
- Kernel autotune freeze: merged
- Scheduler smoke enabled: PR open
- Perf-canary: running now (Marcus)
- Release notes generator: dry run ok w/ compat stub
- Open blocker: signing key access for release branches (not required for RC but required for GA)

Marcus Reed: Perf run kicked off. ETA ~90 min.

Sean Gallagher: If perf gate fails due to runner noise, please capture runner ID + contention. We need data for capacity ticket.

Devon Price: For (9) Private installer + Helm sanity: I did a quick check—helm chart expects tag format `1.18.1-lts` not `1.18.1-lts-rc1`. For pilot we might need to document RC tags as “nonstandard.”

Connor O'Brien: Good catch. For RC we can keep `-rc1` but don’t route private customers to it via installer. We’ll only share RC artifacts internally + pilot accounts via manual instructions.

Elliot Price: I’ll add a line in the internal RC notes: “RC tags not supported by Private installer/Helm pinning yet.”

Sahana Iyer: Signing: I’ll work with infra after perf run. If we can’t get keys today, we proceed with checksums + provenance metadata only.

buildkite-bot: Perf-canary summary artifact uploaded: perf-canary/runtime-1.18-lts/rc1

Marcus Reed: Perf results: p50 within +0.4%, p95 +1.1% (ok), throughput -0.8% (ok). No new OOMs. One flaky run on A100 long-context; reran and it passed.

Sean Gallagher: I’m good w/ this for RC. Please note the flake and rerun in the release checklist.

Connor O'Brien: Great. Next steps today:
- Merge scheduler smoke enable PR
- Land batching-disable default for LTS
- Cut RC2 w/ those changes
- Kira/Elliot generate internal notes + link checksums

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler smoke PR merged.

buildkite-bot: PR merged into runtime-1.18-lts: enable scheduler-smoke on release branches

Hiro Tanaka: Batching-disable PR up. It flips default off on LTS only. Includes note about potential throughput tradeoff.

Connor O'Brien: Reviewing now. If tests green we’ll merge and cut RC2.

Talia Benson: EOD reminder: please drop final “who did what + remaining blockers” so we can convert into the formal runbook later.

source.text:
Connor O'Brien: We need to land on LTS branch/tag naming today-ish. :grimacing: Options being tossed around: `runtime-1.18-lts` branch vs `release/1.18` branch, and tag formats like `1.18.1-lts` vs `v1.18.1+lts`. Main constraint: Private installer + Helm pinning semantics. Anyone have strong opinions / gotchas?

Sahana Iyer: From CI side: I’d prefer a predictable branch glob we can target in workflows. `runtime-*-lts` is easy. `release/*` is already used by some legacy jobs and I’m worried we’ll accidentally pick up non-LTS release branches.

Bruno Silva: Private installer: we currently parse runtime version as semver in a couple places (upgrade plan + “is upgrade allowed” checks). Suffix `-lts` is technically semver prerelease, which sorts *lower* than the base version.
Bruno Silva: Example: `1.18.1-lts` < `1.18.1` per semver rules. That might be fine, but we need to make sure we never compare LTS tags against non-LTS tags and conclude “downgrade”.

Noah Patel: +1 on not mixing comparisons. Also “+lts” is semver build metadata; semver says build metadata should be ignored for precedence. That means `1.18.1+lts` compares equal to `1.18.1`, which is worse for upgrade logic.

Vivek Kulkarni: Private packaging POV: customers will ask for “pin to LTS patch 1.18.1”. If we do `+lts` we lose a unique sortable identifier. I’d rather make the LTS bit explicit.

Marissa Cole: From product/docs/customer angle: `1.18.1-lts` is easy to communicate. “+lts” is going to confuse basically everyone.

Elliot Price: Release notes generator wants a stable way to infer “this is LTS” without having to maintain a manual list. Suffix is easiest. If it’s in the tag, it’s in the URL and the notes can show “LTS Patch”.

Rafael Mendes: SRE question: how do we prevent folks from accidentally deploying LTS into Hosted where we want mainline? (I know it’s mostly config, but naming helps.) I like branch clearly being LTS-specific.

Connor O'Brien: So seems like `+lts` is out. Remaining debate: branch name and tag prefix.

Sahana Iyer: For branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` is explicit and matches the repo name. Also we can protect it and require approvals.

Bruno Silva: Can we do git tag `runtime-1.18.1-lts` vs `v1.18.1-lts`? For the installer we care more about the runtime *image tag* than the git tag, but we do surface git tags in some audit output.

Noah Patel: `v` prefix is conventional for git tags. But image tags usually don’t include `v`.

Vivek Kulkarni: Installer expectation today:
- image tag like `redwood-runtime:1.18.1` (string, but semver-ish)
- chart values sometimes reference it directly
If we go with `1.18.1-lts` as image tag, that’s fine as a string. The parsing is where we need to be careful.

Bruno Silva: Yeah K8s doesn’t care. Our installer comparison logic does. I can adjust to treat `-lts` as a channel suffix and compare base versions separately.

Marissa Cole: How does it show up in Console? If we have a “channel selector” eventually, it can say LTS and show `1.18.1` underneath, but for now most customers will be copy/pasting tags from docs.

Elliot Price: And release notes URL will likely key on full patch identifier. So we want `1.18.1-lts` as the canonical patch string.

Connor O'Brien: One worry: semver prerelease semantics make `1.18.1-lts` look “less stable” than `1.18.1` to tooling.

Sahana Iyer: True, but we’re not using semver precedence for the docker registry or git hosting. It’s mostly our internal comparisons + maybe Helm.

Bruno Silva: Helm chart `appVersion` is semver validated in some tooling (not all). But the image tag is just a string. If we set chart `appVersion: 1.18.1` and image tag `1.18.1-lts`, that might be clean.

Noah Patel: That’s a good separation:
- “Runtime base version” (semver) = `1.18.1`
- “Channel build identifier” (string) = `1.18.1-lts`

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also helps if we want to say “this LTS patch is based on mainline 1.18.1 + backports” without claiming it’s prerelease.

Talia Benson: Jumping in (sorry late). We need something we can write into the ADR and reuse across docs, support playbook, and installer. If we do base version + channel suffix, we should define it explicitly.

Connor O'Brien: Proposal:
- Branch: `runtime-<MAJOR>.<MINOR>-lts` (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`)
- Git tags: `v<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts`)
- Runtime image tags: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`)
- Helm `appVersion`: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>` (no suffix)
- Private installer: pin image tag; compare base semver only; treat `-lts` as a channel indicator
Thoughts?

Bruno Silva: Works for me if we commit to the installer change: never compare `1.18.1-lts` directly as semver. Compare `1.18.1` and then enforce that channel doesn’t change unless user opts in.

Sahana Iyer: CI ok. We can add workflows for branches matching `runtime-*-lts` and tags matching `v*-lts`.

Elliot Price: Release notes generator can detect `-lts` in tag name and switch to LTS template.

Marissa Cole: Customer comms ok. “Install runtime 1.18.1-lts” reads clearly.

Rafael Mendes: For SRE rollouts: can we also publish a “moving” tag like `1.18-lts` that always points to latest patch? Some customers will ask.

Connor O'Brien: I’m hesitant for the first pass. Moving tags are convenient but scary for auditability. Maybe we can add later as an opt-in alias.

Vivek Kulkarni: Agree. Private customers usually want explicit pinning. If we add moving tag later, it should be documented as “not recommended for regulated”.

Talia Benson: Action items:
- Connor to update ENG-4822 / ADR draft with the above naming.
- Bruno to file/track installer parsing update (pinning semantics + channel lock).
- Sahana to update CI branch/tag globs.

Connor O'Brien: Cool. I’ll post summary in #eng-releases and link the ticket. :thumbsup:

Sahana Iyer: :thumbsup:

Bruno Silva: :+1: I’ll start a quick spike on the semver parsing changes and report back.

Elliot Price: Once the tag is final, I’ll update the release notes template to show the canonical patch string exactly (incl `-lts`).

Marissa Cole: How does it show up in Console? If we have a “channel selector” eventually, it can say LTS and show `1.18.1` underneath, but for now most customers will be copy/pasting tags from docs.

Elliot Price: And release notes URL will likely key on full patch identifier. So we want `1.18.1-lts` as the canonical patch string.

Connor O'Brien: One worry: semver prerelease semantics make `1.18.1-lts` look “less stable” than `1.18.1` to tooling.

Sahana Iyer: True, but we’re not using semver precedence for the docker registry or git hosting. It’s mostly our internal comparisons + maybe Helm.

Bruno Silva: Helm chart `appVersion` is semver validated in some tooling (not all). But the image tag is just a string. If we set chart `appVersion: 1.18.1` and image tag `1.18.1-lts`, that might be clean.

Noah Patel: That’s a good separation:
- “Runtime base version” (semver) = `1.18.1`
- “Channel build identifier” (string) = `1.18.1-lts`

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also helps if we want to say “this LTS patch is based on mainline 1.18.1 + backports” without claiming it’s prerelease.

Talia Benson: Jumping in (sorry late). We need something we can write into the ADR and reuse across docs, support playbook, and installer. If we do base version + channel suffix, we should define it explicitly.

Connor O'Brien: Proposal:
- Branch: `runtime-<MAJOR>.<MINOR>-lts` (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`)
- Git tags: `v<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts`)
- Runtime image tags: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`)
- Helm `appVersion`: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>` (no suffix)
- Private installer: pin image tag; compare base semver only; treat `-lts` as a channel indicator
Thoughts?

Bruno Silva: Works for me if we commit to the installer change: never compare `1.18.1-lts` directly as semver. Compare `1.18.1` and then enforce that channel doesn’t change unless user opts in.

Sahana Iyer: CI ok. We can add workflows for branches matching `runtime-*-lts` and tags matching `v*-lts`.

Elliot Price: Release notes generator can detect `-lts` in tag name and switch to LTS template.

Marissa Cole: Customer comms ok. “Install runtime 1.18.1-lts” reads clearly.

Rafael Mendes: For SRE rollouts: can we also publish a “moving” tag like `1.18-lts` that always points to latest patch? Some customers will ask.

Connor O'Brien: I’m hesitant for the first pass. Moving tags are convenient but scary for auditability. Maybe we can add later as an opt-in alias.

Vivek Kulkarni: Agree. Private customers usually want explicit pinning. If we add moving tag later, it should be documented as “not recommended for regulated”.

Talia Benson: Action items:
- Connor to update ENG-4822 / ADR draft with the above naming.
- Bruno to file/track installer parsing update (pinning semantics + channel lock).
- Sahana to update CI branch/tag globs.

Connor O'Brien: Cool. I’ll post summary in #eng-releases and link the ticket. :thumbsup:

Sahana Iyer: :thumbsup:

Bruno Silva: :+1: I’ll start a quick spike on the semver parsing changes and report back.

Elliot Price: Once the tag is final, I’ll update the release notes template to show the canonical patch string exactly (incl `-lts`).
text:
Connor O'Brien: We need to land on LTS branch/tag naming today-ish. :grimacing: Options being tossed around: `runtime-1.18-lts` branch vs `release/1.18` branch, and tag formats like `1.18.1-lts` vs `v1.18.1+lts`. Main constraint: Private installer + Helm pinning semantics. Anyone have strong opinions / gotchas?

Sahana Iyer: From CI side: I’d prefer a predictable branch glob we can target in workflows. `runtime-*-lts` is easy. `release/*` is already used by some legacy jobs and I’m worried we’ll accidentally pick up non-LTS release branches.

Bruno Silva: Private installer: we currently parse runtime version as semver in a couple places (upgrade plan + “is upgrade allowed” checks). Suffix `-lts` is technically semver prerelease, which sorts *lower* than the base version.
Bruno Silva: Example: `1.18.1-lts` < `1.18.1` per semver rules. That might be fine, but we need to make sure we never compare LTS tags against non-LTS tags and conclude “downgrade”.

Noah Patel: +1 on not mixing comparisons. Also “+lts” is semver build metadata; semver says build metadata should be ignored for precedence. That means `1.18.1+lts` compares equal to `1.18.1`, which is worse for upgrade logic.

Vivek Kulkarni: Private packaging POV: customers will ask for “pin to LTS patch 1.18.1”. If we do `+lts` we lose a unique sortable identifier. I’d rather make the LTS bit explicit.

Marissa Cole: From product/docs/customer angle: `1.18.1-lts` is easy to communicate. “+lts” is going to confuse basically everyone.

Elliot Price: Release notes generator wants a stable way to infer “this is LTS” without having to maintain a manual list. Suffix is easiest. If it’s in the tag, it’s in the URL and the notes can show “LTS Patch”.

Rafael Mendes: SRE question: how do we prevent folks from accidentally deploying LTS into Hosted where we want mainline? (I know it’s mostly config, but naming helps.) I like branch clearly being LTS-specific.

Connor O'Brien: So seems like `+lts` is out. Remaining debate: branch name and tag prefix.

Sahana Iyer: For branch: `runtime-1.18-lts` is explicit and matches the repo name. Also we can protect it and require approvals.

Bruno Silva: Can we do git tag `runtime-1.18.1-lts` vs `v1.18.1-lts`? For the installer we care more about the runtime *image tag* than the git tag, but we do surface git tags in some audit output.

Noah Patel: `v` prefix is conventional for git tags. But image tags usually don’t include `v`.

Vivek Kulkarni: Installer expectation today:
- image tag like `redwood-runtime:1.18.1` (string, but semver-ish)
- chart values sometimes reference it directly
If we go with `1.18.1-lts` as image tag, that’s fine as a string. The parsing is where we need to be careful.

Bruno Silva: Yeah K8s doesn’t care. Our installer comparison logic does. I can adjust to treat `-lts` as a channel suffix and compare base versions separately.

Marissa Cole: How does it show up in Console? If we have a “channel selector” eventually, it can say LTS and show `1.18.1` underneath, but for now most customers will be copy/pasting tags from docs.

Elliot Price: And release notes URL will likely key on full patch identifier. So we want `1.18.1-lts` as the canonical patch string.

Connor O'Brien: One worry: semver prerelease semantics make `1.18.1-lts` look “less stable” than `1.18.1` to tooling.

Sahana Iyer: True, but we’re not using semver precedence for the docker registry or git hosting. It’s mostly our internal comparisons + maybe Helm.

Bruno Silva: Helm chart `appVersion` is semver validated in some tooling (not all). But the image tag is just a string. If we set chart `appVersion: 1.18.1` and image tag `1.18.1-lts`, that might be clean.

Noah Patel: That’s a good separation:
- “Runtime base version” (semver) = `1.18.1`
- “Channel build identifier” (string) = `1.18.1-lts`

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also helps if we want to say “this LTS patch is based on mainline 1.18.1 + backports” without claiming it’s prerelease.

Talia Benson: Jumping in (sorry late). We need something we can write into the ADR and reuse across docs, support playbook, and installer. If we do base version + channel suffix, we should define it explicitly.

Connor O'Brien: Proposal:
- Branch: `runtime-<MAJOR>.<MINOR>-lts` (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts`)
- Git tags: `v<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts`)
- Runtime image tags: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>-lts` (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`)
- Helm `appVersion`: `<MAJOR>.<MINOR>.<PATCH>` (no suffix)
- Private installer: pin image tag; compare base semver only; treat `-lts` as a channel indicator
Thoughts?

Vivek Kulkarni: Agree. Private customers usually want explicit pinning. If we add moving tag later, it should be documented as “not recommended for regulated”.
